title: Choosing the Right Emergency Shelter Type
summary: Three main shelter types — tarp, natural lean-to, and debris hut — have very different trade-offs in speed, warmth, and required materials. Choosing the wrong type wastes critical time or leaves you cold overnight.
quickAnswer: Use a tarp if you have one; build a lean-to if you need shelter fast and can make a fire; build a debris hut if temperatures are dangerously cold and fire is not possible.
whenToUse: You are deciding which shelter to build and have multiple options available
You want to understand the trade-offs between shelter types before an emergency
You are teaching others shelter selection principles
Conditions have changed and you are reconsidering your original shelter choice
preferredAction: If you have a tarp: rig it immediately (fastest rain protection). If no tarp and temperatures are dangerous: build a debris hut. If no tarp and you have fire: build a natural lean-to.
backupAction: In arid, alpine, or snow-covered terrain with no leaf litter: use a lean-to with emergency blanket insulation or seek natural windbreak (rock, fallen log) rather than attempting a debris hut.
warnings: No shelter type compensates for inadequate ground insulation — sleeping on bare ground causes rapid conductive heat loss regardless of the shelter overhead.
A lean-to without fire is inadequate for survival in temperatures below 50°F.
A debris hut requires abundant dry leaf litter; attempting to build one in arid or alpine terrain without suitable materials wastes time that could be spent on fire-making or signaling.
Building any shelter larger than you need reduces its ability to retain body heat.
whatNotToDo: Do not spend time on a debris hut if you cannot find at least arm's-length (3 feet) depth of dry leaves nearby — the material requirement is non-negotiable.
Do not orient a lean-to into the prevailing wind — it will funnel cold air and rain into the shelter.
Do not assume a tarp alone provides warmth — it sheds rain but does not insulate; pair it with ground debris or sleeping gear in cold conditions.
redFlags: You chose a lean-to but cannot build or maintain a fire — switch to debris hut construction or seek better natural windbreak immediately.
Night is approaching and your shelter is still incomplete — prioritize finishing the floor insulation above all else; lying on bare ground will cause hypothermia even in mild temperatures.
preparednessTips: Carry a tarp (at minimum 8x10 feet) and 20 feet of paracord — they provide the fastest shelter setup in almost any environment.
Know how to identify leaf-litter-rich terrain (deciduous forests) vs. environments where a debris hut won't work (desert, tundra, alpine above treeline).
Practice both a lean-to and a debris hut in good conditions so you can execute either under stress.
An emergency mylar blanket (2 oz) dramatically improves the warmth of any improvised shelter when used as a ground layer or wrapped around your body inside.

sourceReferences.title: U.S. Army Survival Manual FM 21-76
